text: The surgery begins with the placement of ports, starting with the primary port in the supraumbilical region using the Hasson technique. Following port placement, the robotic system is docked.

The surgeon observes post-operative changes, noting adhesions between the vaginal vault and the sigmoid colon. Using monopolar scissors, the adhesions are carefully released. Additional adhesions between the sigmoid colon and the left lateral pelvic wall are also released with the same instrument.

The surgeon opens the right retroperitoneal space, identifying the iliac vessel and ureter. Dissection continues to create the paravesical and pararectal spaces, focusing on the lateral paravesical and lateral pararectal areas.

The surgeon visualizes the right uterine artery after creating the pelvic spaces. The uterine artery is traced to its origin, skeletonized, and then ligated using a ligar clip. It is subsequently cauterized and cut. The developed spaces in the pelvic region are clearly visible.

The surgeon clamps the right infundibulopelvic ligament using a ligatril, then cauterizes and cuts it. The ureter is dissected further up to the ureteric tunnel. The medial pararectal space is created, and dissection proceeds downward towards the rectum, creating the pre-rectal space. The round ligament is cauterized and cut, followed by dissection of the anterior leaf of the broad ligament, continuing as bladder dissection from the lateral aspect. The medial paravisical space and lateral parametrium are visualized.

The surgeon opens the left retroperitoneal space, identifying the ureter and iliac vessels. The pelvic spaces are created on the left side, mirroring the steps performed on the right. The left uterine artery is traced to its origin, ligated with a clip, cauterized, and cut, revealing the developed pelvic spaces on the left.

The surgeon cauterizes and cuts the left infundibulopelvic ligament after placing a ligar clip. The peritoneal layer and infundibulopelvic ligament are dissected downwards, creating the left medial pararectal space and extending the dissection to the rectum, joining the prerectal space. The anterior leaf of the left broad ligament is dissected, and the bladder dissection is performed from the lateral aspect on the left side. The round ligament is also cauterized and cut.

The lateral parametrium, also known as the paracervix, is identified after the development of the paravesical and pararectal spaces. It is visible as the connective tissue between the parietal pelvic fascia and the visceral pelvic fascia.

The surgeon initiates bladder dissection from the lateral aspect using a Mecarni tube as a vaginal probe. The bladder peritoneum is identified and incised, followed by dissection of the bladder inferiorly down to the level of the middle third of the vagina.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the bladder pillar, joining the vesico-vaginal space to the paravesical space, effectively separating the bladder from the anterior vaginal wall. The ureters are carefully mobilized from their attachments and separated from the medial leaf of the peritoneum, dissecting down to the ureteric tunnel and continuing to their entry into the bladder.

The surgeon excises the lateral parametrium after isolating the ureter. The procedure continues with an upper vaginectomy, excising the vagina with an adequate margin. On the right side, the bladder pillar is dissected, and the paravaginal and vesicovaginal spaces are joined. The ureter is isolated using an additional energy source, a liver shell, through the assistance port. The lateral parametrium is excised, and the vaginectomy proceeds, joining the previous incision to excise the specimen.

The surgical specimen is delivered vaginally using an incarnate tube. The surgeon performs a radical pelvic lymphadenectomy bilaterally, extending from the bifurcation of the common iliac artery cranially to the circumflex iliac vessel and the cupous ligament caudally. The dissection margins include the external iliac vessels laterally and the umbilical artery medially. Lymph nodes from the obturator space are removed and delivered vaginally.

The surgeon closes the vaginal vault using a 3-0 V-lock suture, ensuring secure closure. Hemostasis is checked visually, confirming no active bleeding. The robotic system is then unlocked, and the port sites are closed.
